title: WILT: A Multi-turn, Memorization-Robust Inductive Logic Benchmark for LLMs
authors: Eryk Banatt, Jonathan Cheng, Skanda Vaidyanath, Tiffany Hwu (Riot Games, Los Angeles, CA)
venue: arXiv preprint, v1 only (submitted 2024-10-14). Comments field / citation context indicate 'Submitted to ICLR 2025', but the ICLR 2025 acceptance/venue string is not itself present in the arXiv metadata (no journal-ref) per the extraction header notes.
claim: LLMs struggle substantially with multi-turn inductive reasoning that requires proposing informative test cases over multiple turns and then deducing a hidden boolean rule over three numbers (Wason 2-4-6-inspired): the best model (Claude 3.5 Sonnet) achieves only 14/50 (28%) accuracy on the full split. Models show non-overlapping strengths — some are better at hypothesis-space reduction (proposing good tests), others at function inversion (deducing the rule from evidence) — and because every test instance starts from an identical clean-slate prompt, the benchmark is structurally resistant to memorization/leakage in a way single-turn benchmarks are not.
novelty: The authors present WILT as: (1) the first benchmark explicitly targeting multi-turn inductive reasoning (gather evidence via self-proposed test cases, then infer a hidden rule) rather than repurposing single-turn benchmarks with a tool-use wrapper (contrast with MINT, Section 3.2); (2) memorization-robust by construction because every instance starts from an identical initial prompt with no pre-given examples, so the model must generate and interpret its own data rather than being gradable via memorized answers to a fixed prompt (Section 1, Section 3); (3) a decomposition of multi-turn performance into two measurable sub-skills — hypothesis-space reduction (inductive, Section 4.2) and function inversion / deduction from evidence (Section 4.3, via a test-case-swap experiment) — that prior multi-turn benchmarks (MINT, Aidan-bench, PlanBench) do not separately isolate. They position CRUXEval as the nearest single-turn relative and describe WILT as 'a multi-turn, more difficult extension' of it (Section 3.1).

protocol.description: Multi-turn interactive loop, NOT single-shot. Each test starts from an identical initial prompt (quoted in full in Appendix A.1.2). Per turn, the model must produce step-by-step reasoning plus exactly one of two actions: (1) propose a single test case as a 3-tuple of numbers (e.g. 'Test Case: ```(1,2,3)```'), after which the harness responds with True/False and a remaining-attempts count (e.g. 'True, 29 Attempts Remaining'), or (2) submit exactly one final guess as a Python lambda function ('Final Guess: ```lambda x,y,z: x < y < z```'), which ends the episode. Up to 30 test-case turns are allowed before the single final guess is forced. All hidden rules are non-stochastic booleans of three numeric inputs, written as Python lambdas. There is no partial-credit scoring within an episode: the model wins only by returning a rule that is exactly equivalent (semantically, verified by a large held-out grid + random floats + hand-designed special cases, Appendix A.1.3) to the hidden rule.
protocol.observation_content: Per call, the model sees the full conversation history so far: the fixed initial instructions (quoted verbatim below), then for each prior turn its own proposed test case and the harness's True/False + remaining-attempts response, up to the current turn. It does NOT see any few-shot demonstrations of the rule itself (no pre-provided input-output examples) — evidence is gathered entirely by the model's own proposed test cases within the episode. Quoted initial prompt (Appendix A.1.2): 'You are an AI tasked with discovering a hidden rule that takes three numbers as input and returns a boolean. These numbers can be any float or integer number that can be typed in 3 or fewer digits, not including signs and decimal characters (e.g. -11.3 is a valid value). In each response, you will provide your step-by-step reasoning for your future plans on this task, and then you will take one of two actions: 1. Provide a single test case to observe the output of a rule... 2. Provide a final "guess" in the form of a Python lambda function... You will have up to 30 attempts at test cases, and you will have exactly one attempt at guessing the final function.' A separate single-turn variant is used only in the Section 4.3 ablation: there, the model is given all reasoning-stripped input-output pairs generated by a DIFFERENT model for the same hidden rule, in one shot, and asked to guess the rule immediately (no test-proposal turns of its own).

representations.variants_compared: The paper does not compare alternative input/prompt representations for the core task (e.g. no ablation over JSON vs prose test-case formatting, no alternative numeric encodings). The one representational variant it studies is turn-structure itself: full multi-turn self-generated-evidence condition (Table 1, Section 4.1) vs. single-turn 'function inversion' condition where evidence (another model's stripped test cases) is handed to the model directly instead of self-elicited (Section 4.3, Figure 3). This isolates hypothesis-space-reduction skill from function-inversion/deduction skill rather than testing prompt format per se.
representations.effect_on_accuracy: Providing another model's already-generated (reasoning-stripped) test cases in a single turn changes performance substantially and non-uniformly across models: o1-mini 'shows highly superior single-turn capability in this test, but notably performs relatively less well when provided its own tests' (Section 4.3); when paired with chatgpt-4o-latest's test cases, o1-mini guessed 19/50 correctly, 'far surpassing the best-performing single model in the full test' (14/50 for Sonnet). Sonnet's performance 'degraded substantially more than other models in the same setting' without its own attached reasoning, suggesting its full-task success depended heavily on its own reasoning trace rather than the raw evidence alone (Section 4.3).

models.name: Claude 3.5 Sonnet
strategies: multi-turn free-form reasoning + structured action tags (no explicit CoT prompting beyond the instruction to give step-by-step reasoning)
models.key_results: Best on full split: 14/50 accuracy, 10/50 approx.-correct, avg. 16.38 guesses, avg. 0.54 repeats (Table 1). Median response complexity: 3 operators, 34.5 length, 0.08 set-inclusion ratio (Table 2). Degrades substantially in the test-case-swap single-turn ablation relative to its own full-task score (Section 4.3, Figure 3).

models.name: o1-mini-2024-09-12
strategies: reasoning model (inference-time compute), same free-form multi-turn interface
models.key_results: 13/50 accuracy, 8/50 approx.-correct, avg. 12.1 guesses, avg. 0.06 repeats (Table 1) — lowest repeat rate of the top models. In the test-swap ablation (Section 4.3), shows 'highly superior single-turn capability' but underperforms with its own tests; scores 19/50 when given chatgpt-4o-latest's test cases (best composite result in the paper).

models.name: o1-preview-2024-09-12
models.key_results: 12/50 accuracy, 6/50 approx.-correct, avg. 8.12 guesses (lowest of top models), avg. 0.06 repeats (Table 1). Median complexity: 2 operators, length 25, set-inclusion 0.01 — 'appears to follow Occam's Razor very well' (Section 4.4, Table 2). Fails an easy rule (x≥y≥z) via confirmation bias using only 9/30 turns (Appendix B.3, Table 9).

models.name: chatgpt-4o-latest
models.key_results: 11/50 accuracy, 7/50 approx.-correct, avg. 14.22 guesses, avg. 0.76 repeats (Table 1). Median: 5 operators, length 39, set-inclusion 1.0 (Table 2).

models.name: Mistral Large 2
models.key_results: 11/50 accuracy despite below-average novelty metrics (Table 1: avg. guesses 26.56, avg. repeats 2.84; Table 3: 142 total repeats, avg novelty 0.13). Authors highlight this as 'a previously underappreciated ability' — strong multi-turn success despite weak single-turn-style novelty/complexity signals (Section 4.1, 4.3).

models.name: GPT-4o 2024-08-06
models.key_results: 9/50 accuracy, 6/50 approx.-correct, avg. 15.26 guesses, avg. 0.52 repeats (Table 1).

models.name: Llama 3.1 405B
strategies: standard chat model, run via Amazon Bedrock
models.key_results: 8/50 accuracy but 9/50 approx.-correct (highest approx.-correct among top models) with low avg. guesses 12.21 (Table 1) — flagged as a model that 'guesses immediately instead of uncovering edge cases,' suggesting improvement possible via explicit instruction to use more guesses (Section 4.1).

models.name: Gemini 1.5 Flash 0827 / Pro / Flash / Flash 8b 0827
strategies: standard chat model, run via Vertex API
models.key_results: 7/50, 5/50, 5/50, 0/50 respectively (Table 1); full complexity/novelty stats in Tables 2-3.

models.name: Deepseek-v2.5-chat / v2-coder / v2-chat
models.key_results: 6/50, 5/50, 3/50 accuracy respectively (Table 1); v2.5-chat shows strong early hypothesis-space reduction per Appendix A.2 ('DeepSeek Chat v2.5... demonstrates strong initial hypothesis space reduction') and has high repeats (489, Table 3) and overfit guesses in the swap experiment (Section 4.4); NOTE Section 4.2's parallel claim ('Models like deepseek-v2-chat show strong hypothesis space reduction within the first few turns') actually names the plain v2-chat model (3/50), not v2.5-chat — the paper is internally inconsistent about which DeepSeek variant this applies to.

models.name: GPT-4o-mini
models.key_results: 6/50 accuracy, 2/50 approx.-correct, avg. 20.36 guesses, avg. 1.08 repeats (Table 1); 'weaker performance in the first few turns, but able to cover a greater range of the hypothesis space by better leveraging later turns' (Section 4.2).

models.name: Llama 3.1 70B / 8B
strategies: standard chat model, run via Groq/Bedrock

models.name: Claude 3 Haiku
models.key_results: 1/50 accuracy, avg. only 6.76 guesses — flagged as failing to use available turns rather than efficiently solving the task (Table 1, footnote 3).

models.name: Gemma 2 9B
strategies: standard chat model, run via Groq

models.name: GPT-4-Turbo / GPT-3.5-Turbo (lite split only)
models.key_results: Lite split (Table 4, Appendix A.4, n=10 easy tests): GPT-4-Turbo 7/10, GPT-3.5-Turbo 1/10 (avg. guesses only 2.9, suggesting near-immediate low-effort guessing).

headline_numbers: Best model overall (full split, 50 moderately-difficult hidden rules): Claude 3.5 Sonnet, 14/50 = 28% exact-match accuracy (Abstract; Table 1, Section 4.1).
o1-mini and o1-preview close behind at 13/50 and 12/50 despite far fewer guesses used (avg. 12.1 and 8.12 vs Sonnet's 16.38) and near-zero repeats (0.06 vs Sonnet's 0.54) (Table 1).

Lite split (10 easy rules): Claude-3.5-Sonnet 8/10, GPT-4-Turbo 7/10, GPT-4o 6/10, DeepSeek-V2-Coder 6/10 (Table 4, Appendix A.4) — 'this much easier split produces a similar ordering, suggesting that the bulk of the separation in our benchmark lies in the easier tests.'

Test-case-swap / function-inversion ablation (Section 4.3, Figure 3): o1-mini scores 19/50 when given chatgpt-4o-latest's stripped test cases — higher than any single model's full-task score of 14/50 — showing composed test-generator+inverter pairs beat any single model.

Response complexity (Table 2, median): highest performers cluster around num-operators 2-5, response length ~25-39 chars, set-inclusion ratio 'best models tend to cluster around an intermediate value of 2-4' when interpreted across the swap experiment; in Table 2 itself o1-preview shows the shortest/simplest guesses (2 operators, length 25) while matching top accuracy.

Novelty/repeats (Table 1, Table 3): worst repeaters are DeepSeek-chat-v2.5 (489 total repeats) and open-mistral-nemo (400), both mid/low performers; o1-mini/o1-preview show only 3 total repeats each across 50 tests.
failure_modes: Doom loop: models (especially smaller ones) repeatedly propose already-tested cases, sometimes dozens of times, often with near-identical stated reasoning (Section 1, Fig. 1 left; Appendix B.1 Table 7 shows Deepseek-Chat-v2.5 doom-looping on confirmatory tests turns 18-30 yet still landing the correct final guess; Appendix B.4 Table 10 shows Claude 3.5 Sonnet re-submitting the identical test tuple (2,3,5) for a different stated rationale, only noticing the repeat after submitting).

Confirmation bias: models identify a plausible partial hypothesis and keep proposing tests that confirm rather than falsify it, echoing the original Wason 2-4-6 result; Appendix B.3 (Table 9) shows o1-preview failing an easy rule (x≥y≥z) after only 9/30 turns because it fixated on the 'all equal → True' sub-case and never tested falsifying variants.

Approximately-correct overfitting to spurious constraints: Appendix B.2 (Table 8) shows o1-mini nearly solving a hard co-primality rule but adding an unwarranted magnitude bound (-3≤n≤3) not implied by the evidence, causing failure despite 'approximately correct' core logic; Table 1's 'Approx. Correct' column quantifies this pattern across models (e.g. Llama 3.1 405B: 9/50 approx-correct but only 8/50 exact).

Overfit/complex final guesses under test-swap evidence: DeepSeek Chat v2.5 given test cases for an even-numbers rule guesses an unrelated complicated relation (y == x or y == z or y == (x+z)/2) rather than the true simple rule, illustrating failure of Occam's-razor-style simplicity preference (Section 4.4).

General task difficulty independent of single-turn strength: o1-mini/o1-preview 'ultimately underperform due to gathering less evidence compared to Sonnet 3.5, which is a multi-turn capabilities failure despite not being a failure on any turn in particular' (Section 4.1) — i.e., models can reason correctly turn-by-turn yet still lose by not accumulating enough evidence before the forced single final guess.
one_shot_vs_multistep: The paper's central thesis is that strong single-turn/single-step reasoning does not transfer to multi-turn evidence-gathering performance, and it explicitly decomposes the two: (1) hypothesis-space reduction — an inductive, cross-turn skill measured by how quickly a model's proposed test cases collectively eliminate a simulated pool of 2000 candidate lambda hypotheses (Section 4.2, Fig. 2) — versus (2) function inversion / deduction — a more single-turn-like skill isolated by handing a model another model's already-gathered (reasoning-stripped) test cases and asking for one immediate final guess (Section 4.3, Fig. 3). o1-mini and o1-preview 'show astonishingly low test case reuse... pointing to their strong deductive reasoning skills' yet 'underperform due to gathering less evidence... a multi-turn capabilities failure despite not being a failure on any turn in particular' (Section 4.1) — i.e., inference-time compute scaling that helps single-turn/multi-step benchmarks (contrast with PlanBench, where o1 does comparatively well, Section 3.2) does not help WILT because 'each turn requires only a moderate amount of consideration' and the advantage is 'between' turns, not within a turn (Section 4.1). Conversely, composing one model's evidence-gathering (multi-turn) with another model's single-turn inversion often beats any single model doing both (Section 4.3): o1-mini reaches 19/50 using chatgpt-4o-latest's tests, versus 14/50 for the best single-model full-task run.

relation_to_candidate.position: WILT sits at the opposite protocol pole from c23 on the key axis: it is fundamentally interactive/multi-turn (up to 30 self-directed test-proposal turns plus one final guess, with harness feedback every turn), whereas c23 is explicitly single-call (few-shot I/O demos + one query, one output, no interaction loop). What the model observes also differs in kind: in WILT the model must actively generate its own evidence (propose (x,y,z) triples) and receive True/False feedback that it must accumulate across turns before the hidden rule is inferable at all — there are no pre-given input-output demonstration pairs for the rule. In c23, by contrast, the few-shot I/O demonstrations of the latent rule(s) are handed to the model up front in a single prompt, and no interaction or self-directed evidence-gathering occurs. Output shape also differs: WILT's output is a single artifact of a fundamentally different type — a symbolic rule (Python lambda over 3 numeric args, or free-form equivalent), individually equivalence-checked against a huge grid of held-out test inputs (verifier: semantic/behavioral equivalence checker, Appendix A.1.3) — while c23's output is the single transformed short string (or a 4-way MC label), scored by direct exact-match against one query's transformed output (verifier: string equality). WILT's underlying rules are also single (not composed of K independently varying sub-rules stacked together) boolean predicates over 3 numbers, drawn from a fixed catalog of 50 (Table 6) with fixed easy/medium/very-hard tiers, rather than c23's combinatorial K-independent-rule-subset design.
relation_to_candidate.overlap: Both are members of the 'induce a latent transformation/rule from limited evidence' family and both are explicitly designed to resist memorization by construction (WILT: every episode begins from an identical clean-slate prompt with no pre-learnable answer to a fixed prompt string, Section 1/3; c23: randomized rule subsets over invented vocabularies). Both frame difficulty via an explicit rule catalog with tiers (WILT: Easy/Medium/Very-Hard, Table 6; c23: smooth incremental ladder over how many of K rules are stated/induced). Both report that the naive/shallow approach systematically undershoots (WILT's doom-loop/confirmation-bias failures parallel c23's 'naive-prompt miss' example). c23's own dossier already names WILT explicitly as a 'generator only, not adopted as task' precedent, citing exactly this multi-turn/episodic mismatch with c23's single-call constraint.
relation_to_candidate.differences: (1) Interaction structure: WILT requires up to 30 rounds of model-directed active experimentation with environment feedback per instance; c23 is a single forward pass over fixed demos. (2) Evidence source: WILT's evidence is self-generated by the model (introducing hypothesis-space-reduction as its own measurable skill, Section 4.2); c23's evidence (the few-shot demos) is generator-provided and fixed per instance, so there is no exploration/exploitation sub-skill to measure. (3) Rule composition: WILT tests one hidden boolean predicate per instance from a fixed 50-item human-authored catalog; c23 composes K independently-sampled rule primitives per instance from a combinatorial generator, giving a smoother, larger difficulty space. (4) Verifier: WILT needs an expensive behavioral-equivalence checker over ~74,000 generated test points plus hand-designed edge cases per guess (Appendix A.1.3) because guesses are free-form symbolic functions; c23 needs only string exact-match, a much cheaper and less ambiguous verifier. (5) Reported ceiling: WILT's best score is 28% even for frontier models with no visible headroom-widening lever besides better multi-turn strategy — a much harsher ceiling than c23's stated design goal of a reachable ceiling via 'state all rules.'
relation_to_candidate.what_c23_would_add: Beyond WILT, c23 would add: a single-call/no-interaction protocol that isolates pure in-context rule induction from confounding multi-turn behavioral failure modes (doom-looping, confirmation bias, evidence-gathering strategy) that WILT shows dominate its score variance; a combinatorially richer, generator-native rule space (K independently composable primitives vs. WILT's fixed catalog of 50 individual predicates) enabling a smoother incremental prompt-quality ladder rather than WILT's coarse Easy/Medium/Very-Hard tiers; a much cheaper, unambiguous string-exact-match verifier in place of WILT's expensive large-grid behavioral-equivalence oracle; and a 4-way multiple-choice variant (candidates each wrong in exactly one rule) that WILT has no analogue of, since WILT scores only a single free-form final guess with no distractor structure.

corpus_claim: WILT is a Wason 2-4-6-inspired multi-turn, memorization-robust inductive logic benchmark; the corpus dated it '2025' but it is Oct 2024 (ICLR 2025).
corpus_claim_verdict: supported. The paper is explicitly framed around the Wason 2-4-6 task throughout (title includes 'Wason Inductive Logic Test'; Section 1: 'Inspired by the Wason 2-4-6 task (Wason, 1960)... WILT requires models to propose test cases... observe the results... and iteratively narrow down the hypothesis space'; Section 2: 'a benchmark for LLM reasoning inspired by the Wason 2-4-6 task'). It is genuinely multi-turn (up to 30 turns plus final guess, Section 2, Appendix A.1.2) and explicitly designed to be memorization-robust (Section 1: 'Since the task always begins from an identical starting state, it cannot simply be solved by memorizing the answer to a prompt'; Abstract: 'each test starts from a clean slate... preventing models from relying on pre-learned responses'). On dating: the extraction header confirms submission 2024-10-14 (Oct 2024), consistent with the corpus's correction of '2025' to Oct 2024. The 'ICLR 2025' venue attribution is asserted by external citation context per the extraction header ('expected citation calls it Oct 2024, ICLR 2025 — consistent, but the ICLR 2025 acceptance/venue string itself is not present in the arXiv metadata... not verified directly on the abstract page by this fetch') — i.e., the Oct-2024-submission-date correction is directly verified, while the ICLR 2025 acceptance detail is unverified from this extraction alone (only v1 exists on arXiv, no journal-ref).
baseline_relevant: Instance count and difficulty dials: full split = 50 rules split into Easy (14 rules, IDs 1-14), Medium (25 rules, IDs 15-39), and Very Hard (11 rules, IDs 40-50) tiers (Table 6, Appendix A.5); a lite split of 10 very-easy rules exists and 'produces a roughly similar ordering' to the full split at much lower cost (Section 2, Appendix A.4, Table 4) — useful precedent for a cheap smoke-test tier.

Turn budget: up to 30 test-case-proposal turns, then exactly one forced final guess; valid inputs are floats/ints typeable in ≤3 characters excluding sign/decimal (Section 2, Appendix A.1.2) — a concrete, reusable difficulty/complexity dial (input alphabet size).

Metric definitions usable for an un-optimized baseline: exact accuracy (rule semantically equivalent per large-grid equivalence check), 'approximately correct' (right qualitative behavior, wrong edge case, Section 4.1 footnote 3 / Table 1), average guesses used, average repeats (tests re-proposed that were already tried, Section 4.1/A.2), and response-complexity metrics (num operators, response string length, set-inclusion ratio vs. ground truth, Section 4.4/Table 2) as a secondary Occam's-razor-adherence signal.

Floors/ceilings observed: best model (Claude 3.5 Sonnet) reaches only 28% (14/50) on the full split — a low ceiling even for frontier models (Abstract, Table 1); worst models (Gemini 1.5 Flash 8b, Gemma 2 9B) hit 0/50, giving a hard floor. Lite split ceiling reaches 80% (8/10, Claude-3.5-Sonnet, Table 4), showing easy-tier headroom is much more reachable than full-split headroom — relevant when calibrating an un-optimized baseline's expected floor/ceiling gap.

Verification/equivalence-checking cost: to grade a single free-form rule guess, the authors run it against a 40x40x40 integer grid (64,000 cases), 10,000 random uniform float triples, and hand-designed special cases (Appendix A.1.3) — a substantial verifier cost that a c23-style string-exact-match design avoids entirely.

Contamination/data-release status: code for the benchmark was NOT yet released at submission time ('We will release the code for this benchmark with the final release of the full paper,' Section 2, footnote 2); the paper argues no held-out test set is needed for WILT precisely because of its clean-slate multi-turn design (Section 3.1: 'does not require careful hiding of a test set to prevent misleading results'), a claim relevant to any un-optimized-baseline contamination assessment.

Response format enforcement: outputs are pulled via regex matching a strict tagged format ('Test Case: ```(x,y,z)```' / 'Final Guess: ```<function>```'); authors note 'after controlling for various formatting eccentricities (Python blocks, markdown, bold characters, etc) that all listed models are capable of providing test cases in this format' (Appendix A.1.2) — a useful precedent for how much prompt engineering is needed just to reliably parse outputs, independent of task difficulty.
quotes: "we introduce the Wason Inductive Logic Test (WILT), a simple yet challenging multi-turn reasoning benchmark designed to resist memorization... the best-performing model achieves only 28% accuracy, highlighting a significant gap in LLM performance on complex multi-turn reasoning tasks." (Abstract)

"Since the task always begins from an identical starting state, it cannot simply be solved by memorizing the answer to a prompt and forces the model to use multiple turns to gather evidence." (Section 2)

"Models begin with the instruction that they must uncover the hidden rule and may pose up to 30 test cases of that rule... After a maximum of thirty tries (or any turn before then), the model may make one attempt to guess the function, after which the test will terminate." (Section 2)

"We release two test suites: a lite split, with 10 very easy tests and a canonical full split with 50 moderately difficult tests... we report results upon the full split for the remainder of this work." (Section 2)

"Claude 3.5 Sonnet narrowly performs the best on this task, with OpenAI's o1 models close behind despite using fewer guesses." (Section 4.1); Table 1: "Claude 3.5 Sonnet ... 14/50 | 10/50 | 16.38 | 0.54"
"When paired with cases from chatgpt-4o-latest, it [o1-mini] successfully guessed 19 of the 50 rules, far surpassing the best-performing single model in the full test." (Section 4.3)

"As in the original Wason 2-4-6 task, we find a common failure mode to be confirmation bias – a participant will identify a plausible hypothesis and continue to propose tests that attempt to confirm it." (Section 4.1)

"Closest to ours are MINT (Wang et al., 2023c) and Aidan-bench (McLaughlin, 2024)... MINT repurposes existing single-turn benchmarks by allowing models to use tools before answering. While the range of tasks in MINT is therefore quite large, strong models can still solve these tasks in few (or one) turns, and the unmodified prompts remain subject to test set leakage." (Section 3.2)

corrections: models[Deepseek-v2.5-chat / v2-coder / v2-chat].key_results: the dossier attributed the Section 4.2 'strong hypothesis space reduction within the first few turns' claim to v2.5-chat, but Section 4.2's actual text names 'deepseek-v2-chat' (the plain v2-chat model, 3/50 accuracy), not v2.5-chat (6/50 accuracy) -- these are two distinct rows in Table 1. Only Appendix A.2 explicitly names 'DeepSeek Chat v2.5' for this behavior; Section 4.2 refers to a different model. Edited the key_results field to attribute the claim correctly to A.2 and flag the paper's own internal inconsistency between the two sections rather than silently resolving it in v2.5-chat's favor.
_verification.verification_note: Performed line-by-line cross-check of every number in Tables 1-6, all quoted strings (abstract, Sections 1-4, Appendix A.1.2 prompt, Appendix B failure-case tables), the protocol_mode/observation_content description against Section 2 and Appendix A.1.2-A.1.3, and the lite-split/full-split tier counts (Easy 14 + Medium 25 + Very Hard 11 = 50, confirmed against Table 6 IDs). All Table 1/2/3/4 figures (accuracy, approx.-correct, avg. guesses, avg. repeats, num operators, response length, set-inclusion ratio, novelty/repeats) matched the source exactly with no column misalignment found. protocol.mode='interactive' correctly reflects the core multi-turn task while observation_content correctly notes the separate single-turn function-inversion ablation in Section 4.3, so the two-track structure is not flattened. Only one substantive error was found (the Deepseek v2-chat vs v2.5-chat conflation, corrected above); everything else -- headline numbers, o1-mini's 19/50 swap result, verifier grid sizes (64,000 + 10,000), quotes -- checked out verbatim.
